extra.reasoning:
The monotonicity property requires that setting pallor=true (all other inputs unchanged) never lowers acuity. Enum order low-to-high: self_care < routine < urgent < emergent.

Pallor appears in exactly one rule: r11 (priority 100), whose condition is the conjunction of all 12 inputs except chest_pain, including pallor, and which sets acuity := self_care (the lowest level). No other rule references pallor.

Consider the baseline input with all of r11's non-pallor conjuncts true and pallor=FALSE (severe_headache, palpitations, rash, fever, back_pain, dizziness, syncope, abdominal_pain, confusion, focal_weakness, vision_loss all true; chest_pain true; pallor false):
- r11 does NOT fire (pallor false breaks its conjunction).
- Several priority-3 rules fire and set emergent: r2 (palpitations), r9 (fever), r7 (dizziness), r12 (abdominal_pain), r4 (focal_weakness). Max firing priority = 3, value emergent.
- Result: acuity = emergent (the HIGHEST level).

Now flip pallor to TRUE, all else unchanged:
- All 12 conjuncts of r11 are now true, so r11 fires with priority 100.
- Priority 100 dominates every other rule (max otherwise = 3), so r11 wins.
- Result: acuity = self_care (the LOWEST level).

Setting pallor=true lowered acuity from emergent to self_care, directly violating the property. The witness_assignment gives the post-flip (pallor=true) input; the violating pair is this input versus the same input with pallor=false.
